typecheck.summary: Success: no issues found in 44 source files

appliedUpdates.description: Created .factory/library/benchmark-conventions.md documenting milestone baseline naming convention, Windows benchmark timing variance, regression gate thresholds, and worktree benchmark gotchas

suggestedGuidanceUpdates.suggestion: backend-worker and rust-worker skills should clarify merge conflict resolution when the primary checkout has dirty changes on overlapping files. Current guidance says 'stash/merge/pop' but doesn't address overlapping file conflicts. Consider: (1) require workers to commit to feature branch and let orchestrator handle merge, or (2) document force-checkout resolution for .factory/ files that are frequently dirty.
suggestedGuidanceUpdates.evidence: cli-parity-test-suite worker (bd4777f4) could not merge to main because primary checkout had dirty changes overlapping with feature branch. Merge was skipped and later handled by a separate lint-fix session.

suggestedGuidanceUpdates.suggestion: Add guidance: lint fixes should be minimal — do not mix behavioral changes (removing dedup, simplifying functions, adding error handling) with pure formatting/import-order fixes in a single commit. This aligns with the existing 'smallest defensible change' principle but should be called out explicitly for lint/format fix commits.
suggestedGuidanceUpdates.evidence: Commit 9d8962c (lint fix for E402 errors) also changed normalize_lines from set-dedup to generator (behavioral), simplified run_pytest_suite (behavioral), and added jsonschema import error handling (behavioral). These 4 changes obscured the actual fix intent.

rejectedObservations.observation: services.yaml check_regression command does not document the --milestone flag
rejectedObservations.reason: The command 'python benchmarks/check_regression.py --baseline auto' works correctly for default usage. The --milestone flag is an advanced option used for specific baseline comparisons and does not need to be in the default services.yaml command.
Already documented — no gap to address.
